speaker: interviewer
text: Frieda Aaron, on February 5th, 1995. You were telling me about your particular pathway to your bunker in the-- in your apartment in the ghetto, through the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. Well, this was in January. This was just before the uprising which was in April, 1943. But I want to go back to some other things. I don't want to forget them. Please remind me about the sewers and about the attics, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Yes, it was a kind of-- it was built to-- to hold 12 people. And it had two levels. It was the basement level. And then under the basement level was yet another level. And that-- and that place, we thought that in case somebody dies-- we were so optimistic, we thought that maybe we'll-- well, not really so optimistic. We're skeptical. There was a measure of skepticism and-- and optimism, that maybe that's the only way. That maybe we'll survive, and that seems to be the only way. That is, to hide in the bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had some food, you know. And on the lower level, we thought if somebody should die, we'll bury that person on the lower level.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had Molotov cocktails. Now, what happened was instead of 12 people, there were scores of people. I mean, the bunker was filled with-- with many, many people. And I understand that although my father was not really a member of the resistance, but you-- you know about the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising in 1943. Nonetheless, I think he was involved with-- with-- my father had tuberculosis already, at the time. He also had wounds on his legs. It was as a result of malnutrition. He had sores. Not wounds, sores. And so obviously, he could not fight. But he-- our-- our bunker became the headquarters for the-- for the underground. You know, for the fighters, for the Warsaw Ghetto fighters. And so they gathered. And I remember there was a girl who wasn't much older than I, whom I see in a white dress. I'm sure she didn't wear a white dress. Maybe it was gray. But I kind of-- as a kind of apotheosis, you know, she became-- and I was-- my-- my goddess. And I was extremely pained by the fact that I was not fighting, too. And I asked for a gun, you know, for a weapon. And there were not enough to go around. so I-- I guess she was-- she must've been older than I was, 14. No, I was already 15, actually. Well, so one day, one of the Molotov-- Molotov cocktails-- you know what that is, it's a-- OK-- exploded. And we all had to be-- we all had to flee the bunker. Fortunately, there-- there-- there were no Germans around.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we made it. My-- my sister lost her voice. And ever since then she has some trouble with her throat. You wouldn't know, but-- I mean, when you speak with her you wouldn't know. And then came back to the bunker. We slept in-- in shifts, you know, because there were so many of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And interestingly enough, there was a French woman who happened to have come into the bunker, too. No, she was not a French woman. She was a Polish-Jewish woman who was a French teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? I'm sorry, it's just my tension. And she-- and we were there, and we were in the bunker about two or three weeks. And during the two or three-week period she taught us French, my sister and me. My father insisted. And he said, in fact, I have it in my book. That he said, if we survive, it will be very useful. If we don't survive, it doesn't make any difference. And in fact, even prior to that, during the period when we're attending the so-called schools, you know, when we went to each other's house, my mother sort of almost pleaded with my-- with my father to leave us alone, because we're hungry. And-- and-- and my father insisted that we study. Because he said that this will keep our mind off the unfolding tragedies, of the horrors, the daily horrors. And indeed, he was right in that. And also, if we survive, there was always the hope of surviving. One doesn't-- one never believes that one is going to die. I shouldn't say that for everyone. I certainly did not believe that I was going to die in the concentration camps, whereas my sister did. She didn't think she would survive. And yet, she was the stronger of the two of us, physically and emotionally. As I told you, she's really an extraordinary woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had French. And indeed, it did-- I'll never forget--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --when I-- when I was in undergraduate school I needed several languages because I wanted to go to graduate school. And-- and my-- my discipline is-- is comparative literature, so you need languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I already had some priming, you know. and of course, the Latin that I learned, that I studied before also was very, very useful to me later. But it was useful in more than a practical way.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was useful-- again, I don't want to use the term spiritual, but I don't know what other term to-- to use. It was-- it was a kind of con-- con-- continuation of my father's values. You know, I-- I-- both my sister and I, not even consciously, my sister's an educated woman, too, we held it in very high regard. And we still do, obviously. So that was important. OK, now I'd like to go back, if-- oh no, let me finish with the bunker. OK, then I'll trace back. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. What happens now? Let's just keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in addition to the bunkers that we had built, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or that we had behind the door that is the hiding places-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --there were other ways in which people-- I'm a little bit distracted because of this. OK, there were-- there were other-- what we did in the Warsaw ghetto was to create passageways from one building to another, because we had-- there was a curfew. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, each building--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, just one second. Can-- maybe we can wait for a second until he finishes the phone call.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if we could close the door?

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got too much stuff here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it would be difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are ready, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pick up again. You were telling me about the passageways between apartments that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Between bunkers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No between--

speaker: interviewer
text: At curfew-- something about curfew time, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, curfew time. Between the buildings because of curfew time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, we were not able to walk on the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I want to describe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what avenues of-- what ways of walking we found, of getting from one place to another. Not just my family, but the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, do you want me to tell you? Are we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't know we were on. OK. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, what was-- see, every building in Warsaw, every apartment building in Warsaw had a-- an attic. OK? So what we did-- and they were adjacent, the buildings were adjacent. The blocks in Poland, as I remember, were much longer than they are here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And each block constituted a kind of square, if you imagine. There's this street, that street, that street, and this street. So we're-- so what we did, we made holes-- not I, but you know, the men or whoever-- from one attic to another. And this is how we're able to walk from building to building. For us kids, it was a wonderful place to play. Now, what I find so striking is that-- that we played under these conditions. That we pretended to smoke. We pretended to do all sorts of things. We were Don Quixote. We were-- we were all sorts of great heroes, despite the starvation. Somehow, we remained children, even though we were not children anymore. OK, there was something. In fact, in the beginning, when I was about 12, you know, this was the first year in the-- in the-- in the ghetto. You know, girls and boys begin to flirt. We even did that under these awful conditions. Now, for people who are not there and who are not familiar with the conditions, it may sound as if things were not so bad. But they were unspeakable. Nonetheless, we pretended that-- that we're children. And we're-- all of us were, you know, essentially adults, because the responsibilities that we had to carry were enormous. As a matter of fact-- remind me again about the sewers, OK-- had it not been for me-- I was a child. And it just-- it was just a coincidence, not-- not because I was heroic or anything like that. I had a friend in the Warsaw Ghetto whose father was a-- a foreman in a factory for Germans. A German factory, rather. And this man-- [? Justyna ?] is my friend's name, or Justine. Her father liked me very much because he regarded me as being gentle. And his daughter was a little bit wilder, so he wanted-- you know, he thought that my influence was a very good one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that served us very, very well. Because at one point in the Warsaw Ghetto, if you did not have a work permit, you could not live. OK? You had to work. And these were slave factories. And these slave factories were established at a particular time.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they became particularly important at that particular time, and I'll tell you about it in a moment. So by virtue of my friendship with my friend-- my-- I remember my father and I were running around, trying to find a place to work. And we couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know? So the reason that I'm telling you this, that I was a child and yet, I wasn't a child because my responsibilities were that of an adult. And so my father and I ultimately were able to secure these papers. And we worked in the slave factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it within the ghetto walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within the ghetto, right. Within the ghetto walls. There were many hours it was was [? half men. ?] We were fixing-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --uniforms, the German soldiers' uniforms that were bullet-- that had bullet holes and-- and-- and such. I wanted to tell you something else about that period. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't-- Was it something about-- you referred to the importance of these factories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, without them one could not work. But I wanted to tell you something else. And I forgot what I wanted to say. My thoughts are very scattered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, in addition to that, the-- everybody probably knows about it. Anybody who knows about the Holocaust and the Warsaw ghetto knows that there were sewers. And people walked through the sewers.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was very, very important, because it gave us access to the Aryan side. Incidentally, the ghetto, as you may know, was surrounded by very thick, impenetrable walls. OK? We could not get in. We could not get out. It-- it's not a ghetto the way you know, we think of ghettos here. A-- a ghetto was an enclosed-- it was a prison, essentially, a huge prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so you know, but people did smuggle in food. As you probably know, you may have seen-- I'm sure that you did see some of the films on television, perhaps even elsewhere. And that-- that was essential. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the-- the sewers were extremely important for-- for passage. I was never in the sewer, but I was, you know, in the attic, walking from building to building. Sometimes, out of necessity because we had to get to another building, and it was dangerous to walk in the street, for two reasons. Sometimes it was curfew, which was quite early. I don't remember exactly when. At other times, it was simply dangerous because you would be picked up. You would be rounded up. So it was essential to-- you know, to have these passages. It just bugs me that I can't remember what I wanted to say. If it'll come later, the memory emerges later, I'll just have to insert it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Yes. And actually, there were-- there were a couple of threads I wanted to go back to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you how you got a hold of the Molotov cocktails.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know how my father and others got a hold of it. And ultimately-- oh yes, this is interesting, too. Ultimately, we were uncovered, where this-- you know, our bunker was found by the Germans, Ukrainians, Lithuanians. They always worked together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ironically enough, it was that little boy, whom I mentioned before, whose parents were shot, and whom the Germans-- well, then let-- let's call them the murderers so we know all of them, OK? They took this child under their wings, as it were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they fed him. And he knew about many of the bunkers. And he pointed them out. Now, you cannot be angry at the child. He was only eight or nine years old, and he was hungry. And he was-- his life was threatened. So it's just-- he probably didn't survive anyway. I'm sure that they must have shot him. I cannot be sure 100%, but I am assuming that they must have shot him. And he-- he-- and you know, he-- he revealed where our bunker was. And a grenade was thrown in, or grenades. And they-- you know, the-- we all had to leave. And-- [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop here and go back to something? OK, now before-- yes, now I know what I wanted to say before. OK, there are two things that I wanted to say, OK. In September, 1942, there was a enormous roundup of all the Jews. The ghetto was divided into a large ghetto and a small ghetto. All of the ghettos were divided. And that was a way to demoralize the people. You know, not to have contact with their relatives. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, in September, the large ghetto, as I recall and as I understand, was subdivided. Was divided further, into two parts. Because most of the people had already been evacuated. They'd been all, you know, sent to the gas chambers, to Treblinka. Mostly to Treblinka, most of the people from Warsaw, as far as I know, were sent to Treblinka. And during that period, we were-- didn't-- we didn't know what to do. We had to go to this other part of the ghetto. The one that became the small ghetto, part of the large ghetto that became the small-- small ghetto. It was on [INAUDIBLE] street, [INAUDIBLE]. And my father thought that perhaps we ought to hide. We didn't have the underground bunker yet at that time. We had-- we were still hiding behind the-- the-- the door. But we knew that it was too flimsy a hiding place, so it was useless. And he was wondering whether or not we should go to where they were sending us. And my mother made the ultimate decision. My mother was often the decision maker, although they probably made decisions together. And we did go that the other ghetto. By the time we reached it, there were corpses strewn all over. People shot. There were 1,200 people, something like this, were shot in one-- during one week. So we're stepping over the-- the-- the-- the bodies of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not with indifference, with horror. And with-- and with-- with-- with-- with terror, you know? Because we knew what was awaiting us. And I remember the feathers were flying, feathers from pillows, you know? And bed-- what do you call it, the-- Comforter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Comfort-- you the comforters, but what are they called, the feather beds. OK? There was strewn all over. And they were bathed and some of them were in the blood, you know? There were puddles in the street. Ultimately, they took us to a building. And there we stayed about a week, and with very little food. And my mother-- I was at the time, 14. And my sister was 12 and 1/2. And my mother wanted us to look older. My sister had long hair. So my mother made a bun. She-- she had braids. My mother made a bun. She stuffed her bosom, you know. Her-- she make breasts for both of us, you know, with whatever she had, rags, so that we would look older. She had some lipstick. I don't know where she found it. She put it on so that we would look older. My mother had gray hair, and we were terrified about it. And I-- I remember she had something that she dyed her hair with. Because to be gray, you-- you probably know that the children and the elderly went first. Well, my mother was a young woman. You know, she was in her 40's early 40's at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in the building for about a week. Then we were chased out of the building. And there were selections, to the right and to the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a little girl who lived in an apartment underneath ours. She was my sister's friend. They were about the same age. Her name was Yanka. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --they were-- she and her mother were in front of us. So the child was separated from the mother. And sometimes, most of the time, the mothers went with the children. And the mother was sent to the other side. Sometimes the Germans deliberately separated people. They were so sadistic. There was so much gratuitous sadism. I guess this disarmed us, you know, emotionally and-- and-- obviously physically, because we were starving. And the little girl, I think she was a year younger than my sister, cried, mama, mama. And that poor woman couldn't go. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] She couldn't join her child. She was terrified, I suppose. It was difficult for us to understand that she let her child alone. But she lost her senses altogether. She really was mad after that. When we came back, you know, after the selections, we came-- fortunately we passed the selection. She passed it too. And we came back to where we lived. And we no longer lived in our apartment. We lived in a building next to our apartment, on the ground floor. This is why we're able to get into the bunker through the toilet bowl. I mean not through the toilet bowl itself, not through the john itself, but they cut away something, you know, we're able to crawl in. But this was not in our former apartment, our pre-war apartment. This was in-- in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the building next door to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you had been higher up before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We're hiding in the old apartment, behind the door, but not in this one. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this woman was mad. In fact, shortly after they were deported to-- deport-- well, I guess. I can't think of a better word-- to Majdanek, she was-- she was killed. I mean, she was mad. They probably would have killed her anyway. She was sent to the gas chamber. But she was mad as a result of her guilt, that she didn't go with her child. She was screaming, crying. She carried on. It was-- it was just awful. And one day in this other apartment, the one on the ground floor-- the Germans just walked in, you know, whenever they wanted to. And they saw us in the apartment. And we're terrified. We thought they would round us up and send us. But you know, you walked into the kitchen when you walked into the apartment. The first room was the kitchen. And I was standing next to the stove. And a German came up to me, an SS man. He looked at me. And maybe I reminded him of someone, some atavistic something or other. You know, some-- some memory of his forefathers maybe, came to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- he-- he just-- didn't do anything to us. He walked out. Maybe I reminded him of his child, who knows? Which was a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] OK, so let me go back to the bunker, when we're uncovered, you know, when the boy showed us, showed the Germans the bunker. Obviously we were taken out. And-- and one of the Germans took me into a-- a-- one of the entrances of the buildings. Because there were four buildings, remember, in every-- every courtyard. And I thought he was going to shoot me. Rape never even crossed my mind. I was too young or to naive, you know, to-- to know enough about it. I don't know what he wanted, but he didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then were sent to Umschlagplatz. This was-- Umschlagplatz, it's a German word-- actually, it was a railroad station from which we were sent to Majdanek. Now, at that point, the whole ghetto was in flames.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the resistance was extremely effective. And the Germans did not believe the Jews can fight back the way they do. And they constituted that a tiny group that is the resistance, of kids, largely, young people, you probably heard about the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising, I'm sure. You know, and [INAUDIBLE] and Antek Zuckerman, and all the others. And they were extraordinarily brave. I mean, you know, language defies our experiences. And it's very frustrating to use language to describe something so unearthly, so-- so-- so-- so diabolical and so satanic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, so we walked along the ghetto. The-- the ghetto was in flames. Everything was burning. The only thing on our street that was intact was the church, across. And I came back right after the war, and that's all that-- that remained, the church. Everything else was in absolute ruins. And they took us to that Umschlagplatz which was, as I told you, a railroad station. And they were put into trains. But in the meantime, my father was-- was savagely beaten by a German with the butt of a-- of a rifle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's a special term for it, but I can't think of it. You know, butt of a rifle. And my father was sickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had-- as I told you, he had tuberculosis. He had all sorts of sores. And they put us into the trains. And then-- now I will tell you about the-- our first concentration camp, unless there's anything else you'd like to know about the Warsaw Ghetto? I was going to ask you the eight-year-old boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but you told me that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you had mentioned before so we got back to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I don't know, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a matter of fact, what I failed to mention is that two of my cousins, the-- the two male cousins who were in Warsaw-- not, obviously, the two who were killed by the Germans in-- in Bialystok-- were in the underground. They were fighting in the underground. And they were both killed. Incidentally, as late as December, 1942, as far as I know, based on my research, there was only one pistol in the Warsaw Ghetto. See, people don't realize how difficult it was to get weapons. And-- and then I think many of the weapons were bought from Italian marauders and other people. Because some people didn't look Jewish. And they were able to escape through the sewers to the Aryan side and the Polish side. And women were particularly effective in this. Because they were not circumcised. I mean, a man, if one accused a man of being Jewish, it was immediately, it was possible, immediately, to identify him as a Jew or non-Jew, by virtue of the-- you know, circumcision. Polish men were not circumcised. It's unlike the United States, where both Christians and Jews are circumcised. Or have been. Maybe the Christians are not anymore, I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mind, in all directions. Where was I? I think you were heading for Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, but I wanted to say something about Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, right. My cousins, yes. But what I wanted to say, is that how-- how did they get the money? You know, those people who were-- OK? We gave them everything we had. Whatever jewelry we had. You know, they came to our houses, to our apartments, and they begged us. They didn't have to. We gave them everything that we had. My father had some purified gold, you know, that didn't have any-- any other metals in it, and he gave it to them to buy weapons. And as you probably know, no one wanted to give us weapons. No one offered us any help. The whole world was totally indifferent to our tragedy. I think it's unprecedented. And I think, perhaps, they have learned to help, like, Cambodia. I don't know how much they helped there. But in other places, there was some help to rescue people. And all the doors were closed to us. And weapons, you know, we pleaded with the Poles to give us weapons. Obviously they didn't have too many either. But they were shipped from England, because the Polish government went to England, fled to England. And they would not cooperate with us. And so-- I mean, we could've fought together against the Germans. But they did not do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, to go back. All right, so then we are now in-- on Umschlagplatz. OK, my father is savagely beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are all terrified and very, very hungry. Then they packed us into the cattle cars. Really, literally, it's not an exaggeration, like sardines. There was hardly any room for breathing. They sent us to Majdanek, which is in-- in Lublin-- Lublin, which is 'Lubleen,' as one would pronounce it, perhaps, in English. Which is not too far from Warsaw. But it took us about three or four days and nights to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very typical of all the trains with the-- with the victims, you know, with-- with-- with those whom they were going to kill, to take these extended, long hours of getting to the place. Because in some way, this disarmed us too, emotionally and physically. So it was easier to push us into the gas chambers, you know? You-- you-- so you don't eat, you don't drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in the train-- well, people defecated, urinate and relieved themselves. Because there was no other place. And at one point I complained to my father that it was very difficult to take this. You know, I leaned against it. He always held my hand. I don't mean literally, but metaphorically. He was always there for me. And I pleaded, and I told him, rather, that I wasn't feeling well. And he was helpless. He who always-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --supported me was unable to support me. And all he could do is kiss me, caress me, and-- to-- and tell me to endure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, as soon as we got to Majdanek, they chased us out of the trains with bayonets. That's what my father was whipped with, with a bayonet. And-- and we ran. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we had with us knapsacks and some clothes. We still didn't know where we were going. Even though, as I mentioned before, my father told me in 1943, January of 1943, that there were gas chambers. It was just so startling. But obviously, I believe children are very much-- quick-- I wasn't a child, 15 years old is not a child-- quicker to believe than adults. The adults, I think, had more difficulties accepting the fact of the gas chambers and-- and crematoria. And-- and the train-- yes. And when I told him this, I saw his face. And to this day, I cannot rid myself of the guilt that I feel because my father was killed in a gas chamber, shortly after. OK, we had to run the whole gamut. You know, we had to run from the trains to the camp. And there were five camps in-- in-- in the-- in Majdanek. Four for men-- four for men and--
